storm.summary_layperson: No storms threatening the cove today — clear skies and dry conditions through the evening, with the next chance of thunderstorms not until midweek.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water at 70°F versus 84°F inland air has built a moderate marine stable layer that would deflect approaching convection, but no cells are inbound today.
storm.detail_html:
The regional storm picture is quiet. KOKX radar shows no cells within detection range and CAPE is just 90 J/kg — far below the ~500 J/kg threshold for sustained thunderstorm development. NWS forecasters at OKX confirm "very little instability with distinct 600–800mb capping," noting that remnants of a northwest MCS moving through Tuesday afternoon are decaying as they enter a suppressive environment.

Two active NWS alerts cover the area: an Air Quality Alert through Tuesday evening (ozone and heat) and an Extreme Heat Watch starting Wednesday, July 1 through the July 4 holiday weekend. Neither is a convective threat for today. No severe thunderstorm, tornado, or flash flood warnings are in effect.

The Riverside cove's protective mechanism is technically active: Sound water at 70°F versus 84°F inland air creates a moderate marine stable layer with a +14°F delta. In a scenario where convective cells were approaching from the SW–NW, this layer would tend to choke low-level inflow and weaken storms before they reach the shoreline. With no storms inbound today, this modifier has nothing to do — but it is worth noting for the heat event ahead: the cove will remain meaningfully cooler than surrounding inland communities through the July 4 week.

Shear is moderate at 30 kt NW (HRRR f01), which could organize convection if instability were present. With only 90 J/kg CAPE and a strong cap, the shear is essentially moot. The most likely scenario for Monday evening is continued sunny, hot, and dry conditions. What would change the call: an unexpected pop-up from intense daytime surface heating — possible in theory with surface temps near 87°F, but the OKX cap assessment makes it unlikely before midweek destabilization.

hourly.shift_note: light SE per HRRR; cove currently still SSW 2 kt — transition in progress
backing to ESE; sheltered by Greenwich Point
E — cove heavily sheltered; may feel lighter than open Sound

wind.tactics_blurb: Cove winds are at or below drifting threshold throughout the evening window — HRRR shows 2–5 kt cycling SE–E–SSW, with RYC now at 2 kt SSW. Racing in these conditions will be fickle; if the start fires, hold center and look for any puff from the S–SSW.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime call: B-light. The deltaT is a robust 14°F (84°F inland, 70°F Sound water), which is normally a strong sea-breeze ingredient. However, the coastal station southerly fraction is only 0.40 — below the 0.60 threshold for a confirmed moderate sea breeze — and the LI north shore stations are showing W–N–NE flow, not the southerly signature of a fully penetrated Atlantic sea breeze. The sea breeze peaked earlier this afternoon and is now fading. HRRR's authoritative station mean is 6.6 kt SSW, but RYC in the cove reads just 2.1 kt SSW and the APRS network mean is 1.0 kt. B-light applies: gradient < 8 kt, SB not confirmed active, wind field fragmented (direction agreement 0.57).

HRRR/obs divergence flagged: +64°. The model is forecasting SE 131° for 17:00–18:00 but current western-LIS stations are at SSW 195° — a 64° veered gap. The sea-breeze maturation correction was not applied (coastal fraction below the active gate), but the trust-obs rule leads to calling the current wind SSW, not SE. For the forecast window, HRRR likely captures the broader synoptic backing toward SE–E as the thermal push fades, but the transition timing has moderate confidence — the cove may hold SSW an hour longer than the model suggests.

Cove geometry matters especially in E flow. The cove opens due south; E and ESE directions are blocked by the Greenwich Point / Tod's Point peninsula to the east. Expect the open Sound to read 5–6 kt from ESE/E while the start line feels 2–3 kt. The most favorable direction for the cove's N–S axis — SSW — only appears in the final two hours at 4–5 kt, still below meaningful racing threshold.

Backdoor check (late June, just outside primary window): KBOS shows ENE 9 kt and KPVD shows E 3 kt, both indicating easterly flow upstream. However KGON (eastern LIS) is WSW 9 kt — not backing NE. This looks like a synoptic coastal eddy pattern, not a propagating backdoor cold front. No directional threat from the northeast for Greenwich today.

Fleet impact: at 2–5 kt in the cove throughout the window, this is drifter territory. Starts may be postponed or abandoned. If racing proceeds, center is the safest position — direction is oscillating widely (57% network agreement) with no dominant pressure side. Any brief SSW puff from the open Sound will favor the west side temporarily, but shifts will not be persistent enough to commit to a tack.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (WLIS) offline — UConn ERDDAP 404; over-water wind and depth readings unavailable
HRRR/obs direction divergence +64° (model SE 131°, current obs SSW 195°); direction forecast for 17:00–19:00 has moderate confidence
